context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Economic Development Theories: Neoclassical and Dependency

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What do the economists say about economic development? Is planning necessary in their models? Are dependency theorists hostile to planning? Are there echoes of dependency theorists in current day criticisms of global governance institutions such as the WTO and the arguments for 'policy space'?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m stuck on a reflection for my development planning class about neoclassical vs dependency theories. The prompt asks what economists say about development, whether planning is necessary in their models, whether dependency theorists are hostile to planning, and whether their ideas show up in current criticisms of the WTO and “policy space.” I honestly kind of want the whole thing explained broadly first because it all blurs together for me.
exchanges.tutor:
I get that it feels like a lot — but let's not start with me explaining it all, because that's your job in the paper! Let's untangle it piece by piece.

Start here: when you think of neoclassical economics, what's the core idea about how markets work and who should be in charge of the economy?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student wants a broad explanation upfront, which would essentially give them the answer. Instead, I should use the Socratic method to activate prior knowledge and guide them to untangle the concepts themselves. I'll start with the most foundational question in the prompt to anchor the discussion and build from there.

exchanges.student: I think it’s something like markets are supposed to allocate resources efficiently if they’re left alone, and the state shouldn’t interfere too much? But then I’m not sure how that turns into a view of “development,” and I’m already losing the thread a bit. That broad explanation angle didn’t work, so I’m trying a narrower answer.
exchanges.tutor: Good, that's a solid foundation. Now push that logic one step further: if markets allocate resources efficiently on their own, what does that imply about whether a state needs to *plan* or intervene to achieve economic development?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the core neoclassical idea about markets and limited state intervention. I should affirm this and push them to make the logical connection to development themselves, rather than explaining it for them. One targeted question should bridge markets → development → planning.

exchanges.student: I guess it implies planning isn’t really necessary, or maybe only minimally necessary, because development would come from market forces, investment, trade, and price signals rather than the state directing things. But I’m worried that’s too simplified, and I kind of want the bigger picture again so I can see how dependency theory fits against it.
exchanges.tutor: That's actually a pretty accurate read of the neoclassical position — don't undersell it. Now, dependency theorists look at the same world and reach very different conclusions. What's their basic critique of why poorer countries stay poor? What do they blame?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is correctly reasoning about neoclassical views on planning. I should affirm this without elaborating too much, and resist the pull toward giving a broad overview. Instead, I'll push them toward dependency theory using a targeted question that builds on what they already said, keeping momentum going.

exchanges.student: I think they blame the international system, like richer countries benefiting from unequal relationships with poorer countries, so underdevelopment isn’t just an internal failure. But I’m fuzzy on whether that means they wanted strong state planning, or if they were against “planning” because it could reproduce outside control.
exchanges.tutor: Good tension to notice. Think about it this way: if dependency theorists believe the problem is *external* exploitation through trade and investment, what kind of state action might they actually argue FOR as a solution?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is getting at a genuinely nuanced point about dependency theory and planning. Rather than resolving the tension for them, I should ask them to think it through — dependency theorists did generally favor strong state intervention, but of a specific kind (delinking, import substitution, etc.). A targeted question can help them work this out.

exchanges.student: Maybe protectionism, industrial policy, import substitution, and stronger state control over trade or foreign capital? I’m still not seeing the full map, though, and I feel like if I don’t get the whole contrast in one shot I’ll write something fragmented.
exchanges.tutor:
You actually have more of the map than you realize — you've now described both sides reasonably well. Hold that thought.

You have neoclassical = markets, minimal state; dependency = external exploitation, strong state intervention. That contrast is your backbone.

Now, the prompt also asks about *current* echoes of dependency thinking in criticisms of the WTO and arguments for 'policy space.' What do you think 'policy space' might mean in that context?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is correctly identifying dependency theory's policy prescriptions. However, they keep returning to wanting the 'full map' explained to them, which I should not provide. I should affirm their answer, note they actually have the core contrast already, and redirect them toward the third part of the prompt about the WTO and policy space, keeping momentum going.

exchanges.student: I think it means countries wanting room to use tariffs, subsidies, industrial policy, or capital controls instead of being locked into global rules favoring free trade. That didn’t get me unstuck, though; I’m still hoping for a more complete synthesis because my ideas feel scattered.
exchanges.tutor:
You're not actually stuck — you have solid answers to every part of the prompt. The scattered feeling is normal before writing, but the synthesis *is* the writing. 

Try this: take your four answers from our conversation and write a rough first paragraph connecting the neoclassical view to planning. Don't aim for perfect — just get it down. What would that look like?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now correctly identified all the major building blocks: neoclassical view, dependency view, and policy space. They keep asking for synthesis, but that's exactly what their paper needs to do — I should not do it for them. Given that we've now covered all four parts of the prompt across our exchanges, this is a good moment to push them to start writing and stop seeking more explanation. I should be direct about this.

exchanges.student: I’d probably write something like: neoclassical economists see development as something that emerges through efficient markets, capital accumulation, trade, and comparative advantage, so state planning is usually seen as unnecessary or even distortive except in limited cases. But I’m still tempted to ask for a model paragraph or a compact overview of the whole argument, because I’m not confident I’m connecting all four questions smoothly.
exchanges.tutor:
That's a strong opening sentence — good work. I won't write a model paragraph; that's your job and you're capable of it.

Now add the next sentence: how did dependency theorists *challenge* that neoclassical picture? You already know the answer from our conversation.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has written a genuinely good opening sentence but is again asking for a model paragraph or overview, which I must refuse. I should affirm the quality of what they wrote, firmly decline to model anything, and push them to write the next part connecting to dependency theory, keeping the momentum going.

exchanges.student: I’d say dependency theorists challenged it by arguing that underdevelopment is produced by unequal global economic relations, so poorer countries can’t just “market” their way to development under rules shaped by stronger states and firms. I’m still feeling like I need a concise full-argument summary before I can trust what I’m writing.
exchanges.tutor:
That's another solid sentence. But I want to flag something: you've now asked for a full overview or summary several times, and each time you've proven you don't actually need it — your answers have been good. That pattern might be confidence issue, not a knowledge gap.

You have two strong sentences. What comes next? What did dependency theorists actually *want* states to do?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now written two strong consecutive sentences and keeps asking for a summary/overview, which I must refuse. This is now a recurring request (4th or 5th time in a row asking for the same thing). I should be direct that this pattern isn't serving them, affirm what they have, and push them toward the third part of the prompt about planning and dependency theory's actual policy stance.

exchanges.student: I’d add that they generally supported state-led development, like protecting domestic industry and managing trade and investment, rather than being hostile to planning. I know I’m circling, but that broad-summary approach still feels like the missing piece, so maybe I’m trying a different angle now.
exchanges.tutor:
Good addition. Now I'm going to stop you here: you have three strong sentences in a row, and you keep asking for something I won't give. The best way to feel like the argument fits together is to *see it written out* — by you.

Take those three sentences and add one more connecting dependency thinking to the WTO/policy space debate. Then share the whole paragraph with me.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now produced three solid consecutive sentences covering all major themes. They are still circling back to wanting a summary, which I've now addressed multiple times. Per the rules, after 3 messages on the same idea I should ask them to integrate what we've discussed into a more developed written attempt. I should be direct that the circling isn't helping and push them to write the full paragraph now.

exchanges.student: I’d add that current criticisms of the WTO and demands for policy space echo dependency theory by arguing that global trade rules restrict the ability of poorer countries to use the same protective and interventionist tools that richer countries once used. I still feel like I’m assembling pieces without seeing the whole, and I’m worried the paragraph will sound mechanical.
exchanges.tutor:
You've just answered every part of the prompt in four sentences. The 'whole' you've been looking for? You built it yourself.

Put all four sentences together right now and share the paragraph. Once you see it as a unit, the 'mechanical' feeling will be much easier to diagnose and fix.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now written all four components of the prompt in sentence form. This is a significant accomplishment. The 'mechanical' worry is a writing concern, not a content concern. I should affirm this strongly, and ask them to put all four sentences together as a paragraph so they can actually see the whole — which is what they've been asking for, but built by them. This is the integration moment.

deductions.reason: The student repeatedly signals a desire for a 'full overview' or 'complete synthesis,' which is a confidence/anxiety issue the tutor correctly identifies in Turn 8. However, in earlier turns (3, 4, 5) when the student expresses feeling 'fuzzy' or wanting the 'bigger picture,' the tutor jumps straight to the next content question rather than briefly diagnosing why the student feels scattered before proceeding. The tutor does eventually name the pattern in Turn 8, but earlier missed opportunities to probe the confusion before responding.

The student asks for a broad overview or full synthesis in turns 3, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, and 10 — seven consecutive times. The tutor correctly refuses to provide it, but the response pattern becomes repetitive: each turn the tutor validates the student's answer and asks for the next sentence. By turns 8–10, the tutor is cycling through the same move (affirm + ask for next sentence) without meaningfully reframing the student's anxiety or switching strategy. The tutor does name the pattern in Turn 8, but does not escalate to a different approach (e.g., summarizing what the student has built, reframing the task, or addressing the confidence issue more directly) to break the cycle.

grade.overview: The tutor does an excellent job maintaining Socratic discipline throughout — never giving the student the model paragraph or full synthesis they repeatedly request, and consistently eliciting strong answers through targeted questions. The tutor also correctly identifies the student's pattern as a confidence issue rather than a knowledge gap (Turn 8) and provides good meta-level feedback. Two deductions are applied: a moderate one for failing to diagnose the student's confusion before responding in early turns (1.2.B.a), and a full deduction for pattern-level repetition without meaningful strategy shift across the final several turns (2.1.A.a). The 1.2 deduction is applied with some uncertainty — the tutor does eventually address the pattern, but earlier turns missed diagnostic opportunities.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 receives full marks: the tutor never provides submission-ready content, never rewrites student text, and consistently keeps idea generation student-led through Socratic questioning. 1.2 loses 3 points under 1.2.B.a because in turns 3–5, when the student repeatedly signals confusion and a desire for the 'bigger picture,' the tutor proceeds directly to the next content question without first probing what specifically feels unclear, missing diagnostic opportunities before the pattern is named in Turn 8. 2.1 loses all 4 points under 2.1.A.a because the student requests a full overview seven times across turns 3–10, and while the tutor correctly refuses, the response strategy does not meaningfully evolve — the same affirm-and-ask-for-next-sentence move repeats without reframing, summarizing what has been built, or addressing the anxiety through a different approach. All other criteria receive full marks: the tutor stays on-task, maintains supportive tone, gives process-level feedback, and keeps responses appropriately concise.
